title: Building a simple shelving unit
subtitle: Mark out, cut the carcass, square up, assemble
excerpt: How to build a free-standing shelving unit from sheet material or solid timber: marking out the components, cutting accurately, assembling with glue and screws, and checking square before the glue sets.
sourceNotes: Standard UK woodworking practice for carcass construction. Collins Complete DIY Manual and Reader's Digest Complete DIY Manual. Sheet-material shelf design from Furniture Industry Research Association technical guidance.

content.text: A simple shelving unit is a rectangular carcass with shelves fixed between two sides, a back panel for rigidity, and a top and bottom. The joints are simple
butt joints
with glue and screws; they need no woodworking machinery and no complex joinery. The critical step is checking square before the glue sets.
Design

Standard melamine-faced MDF or chipboard sheet is 18 mm thick. For a unit 300 mm deep and 900 mm wide: two side panels 18 mm × 300 mm × unit height; one top and one bottom 18 mm × 300 mm × 864 mm (900 mm minus two side panel thicknesses); internal shelves at the same width; a 4 to 6 mm plywood back panel to keep the carcass square.
Cutting the components
Step 1, mark out and cut the panels.

Mark each panel on the sheet material using a combination square and pencil. Cut with the jigsaw supported on a straightedge guide clamped to the sheet. Cut on the face-up side for melamine; on the face-down side for veneered ply, since the jigsaw blade cuts on the upstroke and chips the face-up edge.
Step 2, mark shelf-pin holes or fixed shelf positions.
For adjustable shelves, mark and drill two rows of shelf-pin holes at 32 mm spacing in each side panel using a jig. For fixed shelves, mark their positions on both side panels using a square.
Assembly
Step 3, dry-fit the carcass.
Assemble the top, bottom, and sides without glue to check the joints fit and the carcass measures square diagonally (measure corner to corner; both diagonals must be equal).
Step 4, drill clearance and pilot holes.

Drill a clearance hole through the panel that receives the screw head and a pilot hole into the panel that receives the screw thread. This prevents splitting in sheet material. Countersink the clearance hole for a flush-head screw.
Step 5, glue and screw the carcass.

Apply a thin, even bead of PVA glue to the joining face of each component. Assemble the top and bottom to one side panel, drive the screws home, then attach the second side. Do not glue the back panel yet.
Step 6, check square and fit the back.

Lay the carcass flat and measure the diagonals. If they are unequal, rack the carcass slightly until they match. While still in the racked position, pin and glue the back panel to hold the square. Allow the glue to cure fully before moving the unit.
Finishing
Step 7, fit iron-on edge tape to exposed panel edges.

Melamine iron-on edge tape covers the chipboard core visible on cut edges. Iron on with a household iron at a medium heat, press the edge with a block while hot, allow to cool, and trim flush with a sharp knife.
Common mistakes
Unit not square after assembly.

The back panel was fitted before squaring the carcass and has locked in the twist. This is very difficult to correct without removing the back. On the next unit: always square by diagonal measurement before fixing the back.
Panels splitting at the screw.
Screw driven into the panel edge without a pilot hole, or the screw was too close to the corner. Pre-drill every fixing into the edge of sheet material; 20 mm minimum from corners.
